name: Azure Functions Development
description: Building event-driven serverless functions in Azure using triggers, bindings, and durable workflows.
key_facts: Functions are triggered by events: HTTP, Timer, Queue, Event Hub, Blob, Cosmos DB change feed
Input and output bindings connect functions to data sources and services declaratively in function.json
Hosting plans: Consumption (pay-per-execution, cold starts), Premium (pre-warmed, VNet), Dedicated (App Service Plan)
Durable Functions extends Azure Functions with stateful workflows using orchestrator, activity, and entity functions
Function App settings and connection strings are stored in Application Settings, not in code
common_misconceptions: Consumption plan functions do not run continuously; they scale to zero and may have cold start delays
Bindings are not required; developers can use SDKs directly, but bindings reduce boilerplate code

name: Azure App Service Web Apps Development
description: Developing and deploying web applications to Azure App Service using CI/CD, configuration, and scaling.
key_facts: Deployment sources: GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps, local Git, ZIP deploy, FTP
Application settings override appsettings.json and are exposed as environment variables at runtime
Deployment slots enable blue/green deployments; swap operation routes traffic without downtime
Always On setting prevents the app from going idle on Standard or higher plans
Custom health check endpoint allows App Service to restart unhealthy instances automatically
common_misconceptions: Application settings in slots are either slot-specific (stay after swap) or shared (move with swap)
WebJobs run background tasks within an App Service Plan's compute but are separate from the web app process

name: Containers and Azure Container Apps
description: Building and deploying containerized applications using Docker, ACR, ACI, and Azure Container Apps.
key_facts: Dockerfiles define container image builds; multi-stage builds reduce final image size
Azure Container Registry (ACR) stores and manages private Docker container images
Azure Container Apps provides serverless container hosting with built-in scale-to-zero and Dapr support
Container Apps environments group related apps sharing a VNet and Log Analytics workspace
ACR Tasks enable building and pushing images in the cloud triggered by source code commits
common_misconceptions: Azure Container Apps and AKS are different; ACA abstracts Kubernetes, AKS exposes full Kubernetes control
Container Apps revision management allows multiple versions to run simultaneously with traffic splitting

name: Azure Blob Storage Development
description: Programmatically working with Azure Blob Storage to store, retrieve, and manage unstructured data.
key_facts: Blob Storage SDK supports parallel uploads, chunked transfers via BlockBlobClient for large files
SAS (Shared Access Signature) tokens delegate specific access rights with expiry to clients
Blob leases prevent concurrent writes; useful for distributed locking patterns
Object replication asynchronously copies blobs between source and destination storage accounts
Blob Storage Events via Event Grid trigger Functions or Logic Apps on blob creation/deletion
common_misconceptions: Account SAS and Service SAS are different; User Delegation SAS uses Entra ID credentials for better security
Blob index tags enable filtering and searching blobs without listing all objects in a container

name: Azure Cosmos DB Development
description: Developing applications with Azure Cosmos DB using the SDK, partitioning, consistency, and change feed.
key_facts: Partition key choice is critical; it determines data distribution and query efficiency at scale
Change feed in Cosmos DB captures all inserts and updates in partition key sort order
Consistency levels: Strong, Bounded Staleness, Session (default), Consistent Prefix, Eventual
Transactional batch operations execute multiple operations atomically within a single partition
Time-to-Live (TTL) automatically expires documents after a specified duration in seconds
common_misconceptions: Session consistency is the default and provides monotonic reads for a single session, not globally
Cross-partition queries work but are more expensive in RUs than single-partition queries

name: Managed Identities and Key Vault Integration
description: Using Azure Managed Identities to authenticate to Azure services without storing credentials in code.
key_facts: System-assigned managed identity is tied to the resource lifecycle; deleted when resource is deleted
User-assigned managed identity is a standalone resource that can be shared across multiple services
Managed identities obtain tokens from the Azure Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) endpoint automatically
DefaultAzureCredential in Azure SDKs automatically tries multiple credential sources including managed identity
Key Vault access for managed identities is granted via Azure RBAC roles or Key Vault access policies
common_misconceptions: Managed identities work in any Azure compute environment: VMs, App Service, Functions, AKS, Container Apps
User-assigned identities must be explicitly assigned to resources; they are not automatic

name: Microsoft Entra ID App Registration and OAuth
description: Registering applications in Entra ID and implementing OAuth 2.0 authorization flows for secure API access.
key_facts: App Registration creates an identity for the application in Entra ID with a client ID and tenant ID
Client secrets and certificates authenticate the application; certificates are preferred for production
OAuth flows: Authorization Code (user-delegated), Client Credentials (service-to-service), Device Code (CLI)
MSAL (Microsoft Authentication Library) handles token acquisition and caching for all platforms
Scopes define what access a token grants; user consent or admin consent may be required
common_misconceptions: Client secrets expire; certificate-based authentication has longer validity and is more secure
Access tokens are short-lived (1 hour); refresh tokens allow silent token renewal without user re-authentication

name: Azure API Management
description: A managed gateway for publishing, securing, transforming, and monitoring APIs in Azure.
key_facts: APIM provides a facade for backend APIs with consistent policies for throttling, caching, and transformation
Policies are XML configurations applied at gateway, product, API, and operation scopes
Products group APIs and define subscription terms; consumers subscribe to products to get API keys
Backend APIs can be Azure Functions, App Service, Logic Apps, or any HTTP endpoint
Developer portal provides automatically generated API documentation and interactive testing
common_misconceptions: APIM subscription keys are different from Entra ID tokens; both can be used depending on policy configuration
APIM caching is at the gateway level; it reduces backend calls but requires cache-invalidation strategies

name: Azure Event Grid and Event-Driven Architecture
description: Building event-driven applications using Azure Event Grid for reactive programming at scale.
key_facts: Event Grid routes events from publishers (sources) to subscribers (handlers) using topics and subscriptions
System topics are built-in for Azure services like Blob Storage, App Service, and Container Registry
Custom topics allow applications to publish their own events to Event Grid
Event Grid guarantees at-least-once delivery with retry policies up to 24 hours
Dead-lettering sends undeliverable events to a Blob Storage container for inspection
common_misconceptions: Event Grid is for events (discrete state changes), not messages (commands); use Service Bus for commands
Event Grid at-least-once delivery means subscribers must be idempotent to handle duplicate events

name: Azure Service Bus and Storage Queues
description: Reliable messaging services for decoupling application components and ensuring guaranteed message delivery.
key_facts: Service Bus Queues support ordered delivery (FIFO), duplicate detection, dead-lettering, and sessions
Service Bus Topics provide publish-subscribe with multiple independent subscriptions per topic
Azure Storage Queues are simpler and cheaper; support up to 64KB messages and 500TB queue size
Message lock prevents other consumers from processing the same message while being processed
Dead-letter queue receives messages that exceed max delivery count or are explicitly dead-lettered
common_misconceptions: Service Bus and Event Grid serve different patterns; Service Bus for reliable commands, Event Grid for reactive events
Peek-lock and Receive-and-Delete are two receive modes; Peek-lock is safer but requires explicit completion

name: Application Insights and Distributed Tracing
description: Application performance monitoring and telemetry for cloud applications using Application Insights.
key_facts: Application Insights provides request rates, failure rates, response times, and custom telemetry
Auto-instrumentation requires no code changes; SDK instrumentation allows custom events and metrics
Distributed tracing correlates telemetry across services using operation IDs and parent-child spans
Live Metrics shows real-time telemetry for running applications without storage overhead
Smart Detection automatically alerts on anomalous failure rates and performance degradations
common_misconceptions: Application Insights data is sampled by default at high volume to control costs; not all requests are recorded
Application Insights connection string is preferred over iKey; it includes the ingestion endpoint

name: Azure Cache for Redis and Azure CDN
description: Caching strategies using Redis and content delivery networks to reduce latency and backend load.
key_facts: Azure Cache for Redis supports data structures: strings, lists, sets, sorted sets, and hashes
Cache-aside pattern: application checks cache first, loads from database on miss, then updates cache
Redis persistence options: RDB (periodic snapshots) and AOF (append-only file for durability)
Azure CDN caches static content at edge POP (Point of Presence) locations close to users globally
CDN rules engine allows custom caching behavior, URL rewriting, and header manipulation
common_misconceptions: Azure Cache for Redis is not just for caching; it supports pub/sub messaging and session storage
CDN caches content at the edge; origin servers still serve cache misses and dynamic content

name: Azure Logic Apps and Integration Services
description: Low-code workflow automation for integrating apps, data, and services across cloud and on-premises.
key_facts: Logic Apps provides 1000+ built-in connectors for popular services without writing integration code
Workflows start with a trigger (recurrence, HTTP, service event) and consist of actions and conditions
Standard Logic Apps use single-tenant model with better networking and runtime performance
Consumption Logic Apps are multi-tenant with per-execution billing; Standard uses App Service Plan
On-premises Data Gateway enables Logic Apps to connect to on-premises data sources securely
common_misconceptions: Logic Apps and Azure Functions overlap but differ; Logic Apps is for workflows, Functions for compute-heavy tasks
Logic Apps run history stores inputs and outputs; disable this for workflows processing sensitive data

name: Secure Application Development in Azure
description: Security best practices and Azure services for protecting application data, secrets, and API endpoints.
key_facts: Key Vault references in App Service and Functions inject secrets without storing them in app settings
RBAC data plane roles (e.g., Storage Blob Data Reader) are separate from management plane roles
Always Encrypted in SQL Database encrypts data at column level; keys never leave the client
Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) encrypts SQL databases and backups at rest automatically
Microsoft Defender for App Service detects common web attacks and suspicious activities
common_misconceptions: Key Vault references fetch secret values at app startup, not on every request; restart updates the value
Connection string encryption in App Service is not the same as Key Vault; App Service encrypts the setting display

name: Azure Notification Hubs and Push Notifications
description: Scalable push notification infrastructure for sending personalized messages to mobile and web platforms.
key_facts: Azure Notification Hubs abstracts individual PNS (Platform Notification Service) APIs: APNs, FCM, WNS
Tags segment devices for targeted push notifications to specific user groups or topics
Templates allow sending localized or personalized notifications without platform-specific code
Installation API (preferred over Registration API) provides better device management at scale
Notification Hubs does not receive replies from devices; it is a push-only outbound service
common_misconceptions: Notification Hubs and Azure SignalR are different; SignalR provides bidirectional real-time connections
Tags in Notification Hubs can have at most 10 per installation without registration API limitations
